steps.voice: Data Logging and Peak Hold live side-by-side in Device Settings. Two tools that solve one problem — capturing what the engine did, so you can analyse it later.
Data Logging. Pop an SD card in the back of the dash, tap Start Logging, and every CAN signal gets written to a CSV file — timestamped, one row per sample.
The rate dropdown sets how often a sample is written — one hertz for casual long-drives, up to two hundred hertz for dyno work. The Max setting writes every CAN frame as it arrives.
Logs live in slash-sdcard-slash-logs with a UTC timestamp. Pull the SD card, drop the CSV into Excel or MegaLogViewer, and you have a full session playback.
Even better — you can replay a log through the firmware's signal injector, driving a whole dashboard from recorded data. Perfect for testing a new layout at your desk.
Peak Hold is the zero-configuration version — every signal's all-time maximum and minimum is tracked automatically, stored in NVS, and survives power cycles. Tap View Peaks for the full table.
Reset Peaks wipes the record. Useful before a fresh session — otherwise yesterday's pull masks today's numbers.
Together these two let you diagnose a problem after the fact — when did the lambda spike, what was the max oil temp last lap, did coolant ever hit the warning? The data's there either way.
